intake.finding: ChromeStatus record created 2026-05-06 by futhark@chromium.org (Blink>CSS owner), classified feature_type 'Chromium catches up', with motivation explicitly 'Catching up with the specification and improve interoperability (covered by Interop 2026)'. A well-scoped CSS-5 parsing/matching delta with a clear owner, an explicit availability expectation ('covered by Interop 2026 tests and shipped in Firefox. Expect baseline within 12 months'), and a generic devrel owner (devrel-chromestatus-all). At M149/M150 the lifecycle stage and decision were unambiguous.
intake.limitations: No Chrome explainer (explainer_links empty); design originates in the W3C CSSWG, appropriate for a spec-compliance delta rather than a Chrome-originated proposal.

incubation.finding: Design was incubated in the W3C CSS Working Group (css-conditional-5), whose editors include Google's L. David Baron and Apple's fantasai. The 30 June 2026 Working Draft already defines the @container grammar as <container-condition># (comma-separated, matching if ANY condition is true) and lists multiple-*-containers-comma-separated-queries tests, so no Chrome-specific incubation was needed.
incubation.limitations: initial_public_proposal_url and explainer_links are empty; incubation was standards-body, not Chrome-led, so there is no Chrome explainer to critique.

prototype.finding: Implementation tracked in Chromium issue 41491726 'Support comma-separated syntax for @container queries', gated behind flag enable-experimental-web-platform-features. The record lists 7 WPT tests (at-container parsing/serialization, comma-separated-invalidation, comma-separated-skip-invalid, container-rule-cssom, multiple-size/style-containers comma-separated). The prototype corresponds to a tested, spec-conformant parser/matching change rather than a speculative API.
prototype.limitations: Could not read the Chromium issue body (issues.chromium.org timed out under the reader); implementation timeline and review states are inferred from search snippets only.

developerTrials.finding: M149 'In developer trial (Behind a flag)' is present in the manifest and record (desktop/android first=149). However the record carries no devtrial_instructions, no sample_links, no doc_links, and no recruitment/feedback instrument - a lightweight, self-selecting, flag-based trial rather than a structured cohort. Knowable at the M149 event: spec WD existed, Firefox implementation was in progress, Interop 2026 had been announced (Feb 2026), and Safari was 'No signal'.
developerTrials.limitations: No public cohort, task-completion, or feedback-findings evidence; the trial was flag-based with no documented DevRel onboarding kit.

wideReview.finding: WebKit standards-positions #659 was opened 2026-05-07 by lilles; 'WebKittens: No response' -> Safari 'No signal'. Firefox is 'Shipped/Shipping' (fixed in Fx151, bug 2019254). TAG review is 'Not applicable'; security and privacy review were both 'Pending' as of accurate_as_of 2026-06-08. An Intent to Ship thread exists (blink-dev/c/d0vAGYKdr-c).
wideReview.limitations: Intent-to-Ship body was not readable via the Z.AI reader (groups.google.com URL rejected), so exact reviewer LGTMs, objections, and risk discussion are unknown; security/privacy review states were still 'Pending' at the cached snapshot.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial configured (no OT stage; origintrial=false; the feature went flag -> default). For a standards-conformant CSS parser/matching delta with WPT coverage, an existing cross-engine implementation, and no field-only uncertainty, an OT would not resolve additional unknowns.
experiment.limitations: Explicitly not applicable; not used to mean untested.

prepareToShip.finding: Ship landed/planned for M150 desktop+android+webview (iOS null). The record's debuggability note gated enablement on fixing crbug 507611251 ('will be fixed before enabling this feature') and documents an accepted devtools limitation: matched-container identification cannot disambiguate which comma-condition matched (needs a devtools protocol change). WPT multiple-*-comma-separated tests pass in Chrome 150.0.7854.0 (2026-05-23). Security/privacy reviews were still 'Pending'.
prepareToShip.limitations: Could not verify crbug 507611251 resolution or final security/privacy review states (Chromium issue body unreadable).

release.finding: Enabled by default in M150 (desktop/android/webview). Press coverage is a single CSS-Tricks 'Quick Hit #156' (2026-07-02): 'Chrome 150 implements comma-separated container queries ... following Firefox support.' No dedicated web.dev/Chrome devrel article, sample, or doc link is present in the record; the devrel owner is the generic devrel-chromestatus-all list.
release.limitations: A dedicated Chrome 150 release/devrel piece for this sub-feature was not found or confirmed; the CSS-Tricks body timed out, so its date/summary come from a search snippet.

adoption.finding: Family-level Chrome usage telemetry now exists and is strong: the cached ChromeStatus web-feature popularity timeline for the ContainerQueries counter (bucket_id 19, a WebDX family counter covering container size/style/scroll-state queries and the new comma-separated syntax) was 22.030809% of measured Chrome page loads at the M149 dev-trial stable date (2026-06-02), 22.478533% at M149 day30 (2026-07-02), 22.569303% at the M150 enable-by-default stable date (2026-06-30), and 22.990756% on 2026-07-21 (peak 23.101289% on 2026-06-09). Base container size queries are Baseline Widely Available (2025-08-14) and State of CSS 2025 shows 41% used size queries, 7% style queries, <1% scroll-state. BUT this family counter does not isolate the comma-separated delta (no exact feature counter; webdxUseCounterEnum null) and the level/trend is dominated by the pre-existing size/style/scroll-state family; the comma-separated syntax only just shipped in two engines (FF151, Chrome150) in 2026 and the feature is not yet Baseline (needs Safari + time).
adoption.limitations: Only the family-level ContainerQueries counter is mapped (web-feature-family, declared-family); it is not exact feature adoption for the comma-separated syntax, and M150 day30/day90/day180 plus M149 day90/day180 are not yet observed. No exact/feature-specific counter, no unique-site, production, retention, or predeclared usage-target evidence. Safari 'No signal' blocks Baseline.

support.finding: Post-ship window is short; no reported compat breakage or incidents. Known minor gaps: devtools matched-container disambiguation (crbug 41491726 note) and the CSSContainerRule.conditions OM exposure deferred to a separate Firefox bug (2022827); the FF bug carries dev-doc-needed, indicating docs work outstanding.
support.limitations: No public support-volume or issue-recurrence data; too new to assess durable support burden.

deprecation.finding: Purely additive CSS authoring feature; nothing is deprecated or removed. tag_review_status 'Not applicable'.

developerValue.finding: Fills a documented authoring gap: comma-separated conditions let a rule match if ANY condition is true, enabling fallback queries for unsupported features (e.g. @container --name1 not-supported(--foo: bar), --name2 (width > 600px)) and OR-ing across multiple containers in a single rule - both shown in the WD and the MDN condition-list example. Spec-defined, WPT-covered, and already shipped in Firefox, so developer value is established independent of Chrome shipment.
developerValue.limitations: Usage of the comma syntax specifically is not separately measurable; only the family-level ContainerQueries counter (~22.990756% of page loads on 2026-07-21) is mapped, and it is not exact feature adoption.

endUserImpact.finding: Indirect only: an authoring ergonomics / progressive-enhancement feature with no direct user-facing surface. Credible indirect benefit (progressive-enhancement fallbacks; fewer JS/ResizeObserver hacks that cause layout shift and hurt LCP/UX per community analysis) but no measured end-user outcome, positive or negative, and no accessibility/performance regression identified (a11y is author responsibility, same as media queries).
endUserImpact.limitations: No direct end-user impact evidence; the benefit is author-side and unmeasured.

adoption.finding: Unscored: the only mapped Chrome usage counter is the family-level ContainerQueries counter (web-feature-family, declared-family confidence), not an exact counter for the comma-separated syntax, so it cannot score adoption of this sub-feature. The family counter is strong and gently rising across the launch window - 22.030809% of measured Chrome page loads at M149 dev-trial stable (2026-06-02), 22.478533% at M149 day30 (2026-07-02), 22.569303% at M150 enable-by-default stable (2026-06-30), 22.990756% on 2026-07-21 (peak 23.101289% on 2026-06-09) - but that movement is dominated by the pre-existing container size/style/scroll-state family and cannot be attributed to the comma-separated delta. Base container size queries are Baseline Widely Available. No predeclared usage target exists (the record's expectation is Baseline within 12 months, an interop/Baseline target rather than a usage target) and there is no corroborating production/retention evidence for the comma-separated delta, so the adoption outcome remains unscored rather than mixed.
adoption.limitations: No exact (comma-separated-specific) Chrome usage counter exists; the ContainerQueries family counter aggregates all container-query behavior. No unique-site, production, retention, task-success, or end-user-benefit evidence; no predeclared usage target. M150 day30/day90/day180 checkpoints not yet observed.

interoperability.finding: Two of three engines shipping/shipped (Firefox Fx151, Chrome M150) with shared WPT tests (multiple-size/style-containers-comma-separated) passing in Chrome 150, and the feature sits in the Interop 2026 'container style queries' focus area co-signed by all engine vendors. However Safari/WebKit is 'No signal' (issue open, no response since 2026-05-07), the spec is still a Working Draft, and Chrome is a catch-up laggard (Firefox first). Baseline is therefore not yet reachable.
interoperability.limitations: Exact WPT interop-2026 pass-rate numbers were not parsed (client-rendered dashboard); the WebKit position may change.

implementationQuality.finding: Core parsing/matching is spec-conformant and WPT-covered (7 record tests plus spec-listed multiple-*-comma-separated tests; Chrome 150 passing). But accepted/reported gaps remain: devtools cannot identify which comma-condition matched (needs a devtools protocol change; crbug 507611251 was gated before enabling), and the CSSContainerRule.conditions OM exposure was deferred to a separate bug (Firefox 2022827). Security and privacy reviews were still 'Pending' at accurate_as_of.
implementationQuality.limitations: Could not read crbug 507611251 resolution or final review states (Chromium issue body unreadable).

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: The internal ChromeStatus record is thorough (summary, motivation, availability expectation, FF/Safari positions, WPT list, debuggability note, devrel owner). External enablement is thin: no dedicated devrel article/samples/docs in the record; only a CSS-Tricks 'Quick Hit' press line; MDN carries the new formal syntax and condition-list example but its prose is stale ('It is not possible to target multiple containers in a single container query') and the FF bug is tagged dev-doc-needed.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: A dedicated web.dev/Chrome release piece was not confirmed; the intent-to-ship body was unreadable.

support.finding: Short post-ship window; no reported breakage or incidents. Known minor follow-ups (devtools matched-container disambiguation; OM conditions exposure) are documented rather than hidden, and the MDN dev-doc-needed keyword flags remaining docs debt. Burden appears low but is unmeasured.
support.limitations: No public support-volume/recurrence data; too new for a durable read.

overall.finding: A well-scoped, low-risk 'Chromium catches up' interop delta that shipped on schedule (M149 flag -> M150 default) shortly after Firefox (Fx151), with WPT coverage and Interop 2026 backing. Real gaps remain: Safari 'No signal' (blocks Baseline), spec still a Working Draft, devtools/OM follow-ups, pending security/privacy reviews, thin external enablement/docs, and only a family-level container-queries usage counter (22.990756% of measured Chrome page loads on 2026-07-21, not an exact measure of the comma-separated syntax) with no exact-feature, production, or retention signal yet. Net: a competent, uneventful catch-up rather than a clear success.
overall.limitations: See outcome dimensions above; intent/crbug primary bodies were not retrievable by the reader.

supports: Feature 6196591858941952 record: name, summary, feature_type 'Chromium catches up', owner futhark@chromium.org, Blink>CSS, M149 dev trial and M150 enable-by-default stages, FF 'Shipped/Shipping', Safari 'No signal', WebDev 'No signals', spec link, 7 WPT tests, finch CommaSeparatedContainerQueries, flag enable-experimental-web-platform-features, debuggability/devtools limitation + crbug 507611251 gating, security/privacy review 'Pending', availability expectation baseline within 12 months.
sources.limitations: Cached snapshot accurate_as_of 2026-06-08; later states (post-M150) not reflected.

supports: CSS Conditional Rules Module Level 5, W3C Working Draft 30 June 2026; @container grammar is <container-condition>#; a query is true if ANY component <container-condition> is true; each condition selects its own query container; editors include L. David Baron (Google) and fantasai (Apple); lists multiple-size/style-containers-comma-separated-queries tests.
sources.limitations: Working Draft, not a Recommendation; subject to change.

supports: Chromium issue 41491726 title 'Support comma-separated syntax for @container queries'; 'Enable comma separated container queries for M150'; I2S thread link blink-dev/c/d0vAGYKdr-c; devtools/Selector() limitation for single query vs comma-separated list.
sources.limitations: Issue body could not be read (reader timed out on issues.chromium.org); all content from search snippets, not the full tracker.

supports: An Intent to Ship: Comma-separated Container Queries thread exists; summary matches the ChromeStatus record ('The @container rule applies if at least one of the queries matches ... fallback queries for features which are not supported in all browsers').
sources.limitations: Thread body not readable via the Z.AI reader (groups.google.com URL rejected); exact reviewers, LGTMs, objections, and risk discussion unknown.

supports: Bug 2019254 'Parsing and matching support for multiple comma-separated conditions in @container rules', RESOLVED FIXED, status-firefox151=fixed, relnote-firefox 151+, added to final Fx151 release notes; implemented by alaskanemily; blocks interop-2026-container-style-queries; CSSContainerRule.conditions OM exposure deferred to separate bug 2022827; keyword dev-doc-needed.
sources.limitations: Doubles as Mozilla's position/implementation evidence; bugzilla page last updated 2026-05-13.

supports: WebKit standards-positions issue #659 'Comma-separated @container queries' opened 2026-05-07 by lilles; 'WebKittens: No response'; labelled topic:css, venue: W3C CSS WG; states 'Shipped in Firefox / To be shipped in Chromium'; no WebKit position taken (No signal).
sources.limitations: Open issue; WebKit may respond later. This is absence of a position, not opposition.

supports: Interop 2026 announced 2026-02-12 by Google, Igalia, Microsoft, Mozilla and WebKit; 'Container style queries' is a 2026 focus area; all engine vendors co-signed the effort that includes comma-separated container query tests.
sources.limitations: Vendor announcement; describes the focus area, not this feature's specific score.

supports: MDN @container reference includes the formal syntax @container <container-condition># and a 'Condition list' example combining size/style/scroll-state conditions; the browser-compatibility table did not render and the prose still states 'It is not possible to target multiple containers in a single container query', contradicting the new comma-separated capability.
sources.limitations: Page last modified 2025-07-27 (pre-dates Chrome/Firefox shipment); stale prose and dev-doc-needed indicate incomplete docs.

supports: Independent community analysis of container queries; State of CSS 2025 adoption data: 41% used container size queries, 7% used style queries, <1% used scroll-state queries, awareness of size queries up from 81% (2024) to 86% (2025); notes container collapse/containment gotchas and that container queries complement rather than replace media queries.
sources.limitations: Published 2025-12-26 (modified 2026-03-27), pre-dating the comma-separated ship; family-level data, not comma-specific; not Chrome-feature-specific.

supports: CSS-Tricks 'Quick Hit #156' (2026-07-02): 'Chrome 150 implements comma-separated container queries as well as support for light-dark(), following Firefox support.' Confirms M150 shipment and the Firefox-first framing.
sources.limitations: Article body timed out under the reader; title, date and summary are from a search snippet, not full text.

supports: WPT test multiple-size-containers-comma-separated-queries.html passing on Chrome 150.0.7854.0 (Linux 22.04) on 2026-05-23, evidencing a working Chrome 150 implementation before stable.
sources.limitations: Single test, single run; the dashboard is client-rendered so aggregate interop numbers were not parsed.

supports: Base container size queries feature: Baseline Widely Available since 2025-08-14; ~19.23% of Chrome page loads (older WebDX snapshot); Chrome 105, Firefox 110, Safari 16 first shipped; spec css-conditional-5; included in Interop 2023; State of CSS 2025 references.
sources.limitations: Baseline 'widely available' applies to the base container-queries feature, NOT the comma-separated delta (which is not yet Baseline); the ~19.23% usage figure is an older WebDX snapshot of the same family counter now reported more currently by the cached ChromeStatus timeline (cstl19); no separate comma-separated entry observed.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (web feature popularity timeline)
supports: Cached ChromeStatus web-feature popularity timeline for bucket_id 19, property ContainerQueries - a WebDX family counter (scope web-feature-family, confidence declared-family) covering container size/style/scroll-state queries including the new comma-separated syntax; ChromeStatus web_feature container-queries maps to this counter and webdxUseCounterEnum is null (no exact feature counter), originTrialCounters empty.

Observed page-load shares (share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired at least once): M149 dev-trial stable 2026-06-02 = 22.030809%; M149 day30 2026-07-02 = 22.478533%; M150 enable-by-default stable 2026-06-30 = 22.569303%; latest observed 2026-07-21 = 22.990756%; peak 2026-06-09 = 23.101289%; snapshot 2026-07-20 = 22.904052%; counter first observed positive 2024-06-04. M149 day90/day180 and all M150 day30/day90/day180 checkpoints not yet observed (null).
sources.limitations: Family counter, not an exact counter for the comma-separated delta - it aggregates all container-query behavior, so the level/trend is dominated by the pre-existing size/style/scroll-state family and cannot isolate the comma-separated syntax; do not read as unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, or end-user benefit. Later checkpoints (M150 day30+) not yet observed as of the 2026-07-22 evidence snapshot.

successes: Clean 'catch-up' scoping: a spec-conformant delta owned by an established Blink CSS engineer (futhark), with explicit Interop 2026 motivation and an explicit baseline-within-12-months availability expectation (cs).
Cross-engine sequencing worked: Firefox shipped Fx151 first, Chrome followed M150, shared WPT tests were authored, and press correctly framed it as 'following Firefox support' (ffb, wpt, cstricks).
A devtools risk was surfaced up front and explicitly gated: the record required fixing crbug 507611251 'before enabling this feature' (cs).
On-schedule, low-ceremony flag -> default rollout across desktop/android/webview with no reported compat breakage (cs, support evidence).
failures: Safari/WebKit position was never obtained: standards-positions #659 sat with 'No response' from 2026-05-07 through the evidence cutoff, leaving a 2-of-3 engine state and blocking Baseline - despite the feature sitting in an Interop 2026 focus area that Safari co-signed (wk, interopwk).

External enablement was thin: no dedicated devrel article/samples/docs in the record; only a CSS-Tricks 'Quick Hit' press line; MDN prose is stale and self-contradictory and the FF bug is tagged dev-doc-needed (cs, mdn, cstricks, ffb).
Formal reviews were incomplete at accurate_as_of: security and privacy review both 'Pending'; tag_review 'Not applicable' (arguably fine for pure CSS parsing, but worth noting) (cs).

Known devtools/OM limitations shipped as accepted gaps rather than resolved: matched-container identification cannot disambiguate which comma-condition matched, and CSSContainerRule.conditions OM exposure was deferred to a separate bug (Firefox 2022827) (cs, crb, ffb).
counterfactuals: If a lightweight, earlier WebKit/Safari position request had been filed and pursued (the issue sat with 'No response'), the Baseline path would be de-risked and the 'catch-up' story more compelling; the Safari-co-signed Interop 2026 container-style-queries focus area was the natural lever to get an explicit WebKit position before Chrome shipped.

If a short web.dev/Chrome release note plus an MDN BCD entry and a non-contradictory example had been staged before M150, the dev-doc-needed gap and the stale MDN sentence ('not possible to target multiple containers in a single container query') would not have shipped alongside the feature, reducing early-adopter confusion.

If the devtools matched-container limitation and the OM conditions follow-up had been tracked as explicit ship-blockers or clearly documented known-issues at release, the support/iteration handoff would be cleaner.

If the run had pre-cached primary intent-thread and crbug content into the evidence store at build time (chromestatuslite or the ChromeStatus API), the retrospective would not depend on live fetch of groups.google.com/issues.chromium.org, which failed persistently under the automated reader, leaving the wide-review/prepare-to-ship transition packets under-evidenced.

If the run had cached the ChromeStatus webfeature/CSS/feature timeline for each mapped counter's bucket_id at build time (here bucket_id 19, the ContainerQueries family counter), the adoption phase and outcome would have reported the family-level ~22.990756% page-load share (and the M149/M150 atStable and M149 day30 checkpoints) with an explicit family-scope caveat, instead of implying no Chrome usage telemetry existed for the feature.
skillImprovements: Add a 'catch-up / interop feature' fast-path in the phase router: when feature_type is 'Chromium catches up' and the feature is in an Interop focus area, default-require (a) an explicit other-engine position-request log per engine with dates, (b) WPT interop-dashboard numbers captured to the evidence store, and (c) a web-features Baseline-status check; surface any engine 'no signal' as a Baseline-blocker risk in the prepare-to-ship transition packet.

Pre-cache primary intent-thread and crbug content (chromestatuslite / ChromeStatus API / crbug export) into the evidence directory at run-build time rather than relying on live Z.AI reader fetch of groups.google.com and issues.chromium.org, which are persistently hard for automated readers; mark each as a 'cached snapshot at <date>'.

Add a docs-readiness check to the prepare-to-ship/transition packet: MDN BCD entry and example present and not stale; a devrel article or release-note line scheduled; dev-doc-needed keyword absent. Flag self-contradictory prose (like MDN's 'not possible to target multiple containers') before ship.

Add a devtools/CSSOM-completeness check for CSS features: when a feature changes CSSOM (e.g. a new CSSContainerRule.conditions) or DevTools behavior (matched-container disambiguation), require either completion or an explicitly documented known-issue before default-enable.

Add a usage-evidence step that distinguishes 'no mapped public counter' from 'a broader family counter exists': when the only mapping is a WebDX family/property counter (web-feature-family / declared-family) or an origin-trial counter, still cache and report the ChromeStatus webfeature/CSS/feature timeline for that bucket_id (observed percentage plus launch-relative atStable/day30/day90/day180 checkpoints) with an explicit 'family/property/trial scope - not exact feature adoption' caveat and the page-load-share interpretation, rather than stating no usage telemetry exists. Scope-tag each counter as exact / family / property / origin-trial / multiple.

Add a regression eval case for 'interop catch-up with one engine no-signal and only a family-level counter' so reports do not over-score interoperability as success when a major engine is absent, surface family-level usage telemetry with the correct scope caveat, and keep adoption unscored until an exact (feature-specific) counter or corroborating production/retention evidence exists.

missingEvidence: Intent to Ship body (blink-dev/c/d0vAGYKdr-c) not readable via Z.AI reader (groups.google.com URL rejected) - exact reviewer LGTMs, objections, and risk discussion unknown.
Chromium issue 41491726 body not readable (issues.chromium.org timed out) - implementation timeline, review states, and crbug 507611251 resolution unverified beyond search snippets.
Exact WPT interop-2026 container-style-queries pass-rate numbers not parsed (the dashboard is client-rendered).

No exact (comma-separated-specific) Chrome usage counter exists - only the broader ContainerQueries family counter is mapped (web-feature-family, declared-family); M150 day30/day90/day180 and M149 day90/day180 checkpoints not yet observed. No unique-site, production, retention, task-success, or end-user-benefit evidence; no predeclared usage target.
A dedicated Chrome 150 devrel/release-note article for this sub-feature was not found or confirmed.
